title: Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill would give Muhammad Ali a special gold medal from Congress after he died to honor all the good things he did for America. Muhammad Ali was a famous boxer who also fought for civil rights and helped people around the world. The government would make the medal and give it to his wife. They could also make and sell bronze copies of the medal to help pay for the costs of making them.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Muhammad Ali Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts about Muhammad Ali. He was born Cassius Marcellus Clay Junior on January seventeenth nineteen forty-two in Louisville Kentucky. His parents were Cassius Senior and Odessa Clay. Muhammad Ali was one of the most famous athletes of the twentieth century. He created some of the greatest sports memories in the United States. He won a gold medal at the nineteen sixty Summer Olympics and lit the Olympic torch at the nineteen ninety-six Summer Olympics. After a successful amateur career where he won two National AAU light heavyweight titles, Muhammad Ali became the first professional boxer in history to win the heavyweight title three separate times. On April twenty-eighth nineteen sixty-seven, he lost his boxing title and was banned from competing because he refused to fight in the Vietnam War for religious and moral reasons. However, after the Supreme Court of the United States made a unanimous decision in nineteen seventy-one, Muhammad Ali's conscientious objector status was confirmed. His boxing license was given back to him and he was cleared of any wrongdoing. As an African American and a Muslim who lived during a time that continued to question his civil rights, Muhammad Ali fought issues of race and religion. He was recognized as one of the champions of the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. Muhammad Ali received many awards for his sporting skills and his support of racial harmony. These awards included the Dr Martin Luther King Memorial Award, the Spirit of America Award, the Amnesty International Lifetime Achievement Award, the Arthur Ashe Award for Courage, the Essence Living Legend Award, the Rainbow Coalition Lifetime Achievement Award, the XNBA Human Spirit Award, the Presidential Citizens Medal, and the Presidential Medal of Freedom. Muhammad Ali was honored by many organizations for his achievements both inside and outside the boxing ring. Sports Illustrated named him Sportsman of the Century. GQ magazine named him Athlete of the Century. The British Broadcasting Corporation named him Sports Personality of the Century. The Kentucky Athletic Hall of Fame named him Kentucky Athlete of the Century. The State of Kentucky named him Kentuckian of the Century. The Advertising Club of Louisville named him Louisvillian of the Century. The World Sports Awards of the Century named him Boxer of the Century. The International Boxing Hall of Fame recognized him. He received honorary doctorate degrees from Muhlenberg College and Princeton University. Muhammad Ali received the prestigious Otto Hahn Peace Medal in Gold from the United Nations Association of Germany for his work with the United Nations and the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. The California Bicentennial Foundation for the US Constitution selected Muhammad Ali for personifying the vitality of the Bill of Rights in various high-profile activities. Despite being diagnosed with Parkinson's disease in the early nineteen eighties, Muhammad Ali dedicated his life to the cause of universal human rights and freedom. His commitment to equal justice and peace touched the lives of hundreds of thousands of people worldwide. President Jimmy Carter asked Muhammad Ali to meet with African leaders in Tanzania, Kenya, Nigeria, Liberia, and Senegal as part of President Carter's diplomatic efforts to promote human rights in the nineteen eighties. In nineteen ninety, Muhammad Ali traveled to the Middle East to seek the release of United States and British hostages that were being held as human shields in the first Gulf War. As a result of his intervention, fifteen United States hostages were freed on December second of that year. In nineteen ninety-eight, Muhammad Ali was chosen as the UN Messenger of Peace. Several Presidents of the United States recognized Muhammad Ali. President George W Bush on November seventeenth two thousand and two called him a man of peace and stated that across the world, billions of people know Muhammad Ali as a brave, compassionate, and charming man, and the American people are proud to call Muhammad Ali one of our own. President Bill Clinton stated that Muhammad Ali captured the world's imagination and its heart. Outside the ring, Muhammad Ali has dedicated his life to working for children, feeding the hungry, supporting his faith, and standing up for racial equality. He always fought for a just and more humane world, breaking down barriers here in America and around the world. There is no telling how many tens of millions of people had their hearts swell with pride and their eyes swell with tears in nineteen ninety-six when Muhammad Ali lit the Olympic torch, because we know, now and forever, he is the greatest. President Jimmy Carter cited Muhammad Ali as Mr International Friendship. President Barack Obama, as a Senator, had a framed picture of Muhammad Ali hanging in his office. Before announcing his intentions to run for President, Obama visited with Muhammad Ali at the Ali Center in Louisville Kentucky. Muhammad Ali encouraged humanity through his perseverance and the support of thousands of people. He helped such organizations as the Chicago-based adoption agency The Cradle, the Make-A-Wish Foundation, the Special Olympics organization, Best Buddies, and Herbert E Birch Services. Herbert E Birch Services is an organization that runs a school for handicapped children and young adults, in addition to a summer camp for children with AIDS. Muhammad Ali and his wife Lonnie were the founding directors of the Muhammad Ali Parkinson Center in Phoenix Arizona. They helped raise over fifty million dollars for Parkinson's research. The Center's mission is to provide excellence in treatment, research, and education for patients and families affected by Parkinson's disease and other movement disorders, regardless of their ability to pay. Muhammad Ali was an inspiration to countless individuals with Parkinson's disease, including members of the Rock Steady Boxing Foundation in Indianapolis Indiana. This foundation was created to give people with Parkinson's disease hope by improving their quality of life using boxing for fitness. Muhammad Ali was one of the founding members of Athletes for Hope. This is an organization created by a few very successful athletes of exemplary character who have a deep commitment to charitable and community causes. Muhammad Ali also established the Muhammad Ali Center in his hometown of Louisville Kentucky. The Center promotes respect, hope, and understanding, and inspires people everywhere to be as great as they can be. A visitor of the Muhammad Ali Center experiences the hows of Ali's life. How he found the courage, the dedication, and the discipline to become a world champion. How he found the conviction to stand up for what he believed. How he turned his passion for excellence in the ring to a passion for peace on the world stage. Like Muhammad Ali himself, the Muhammad Ali Center focuses on what brings individuals together, not what sets them apart. It is a global gathering place to which people can come, both online and in person, to learn, share, and celebrate our commonalities as human beings and to formulate ways of advancing humanity. Muhammad Ali helped to provide more than twenty-two million aid packets to assist people in need. Until the last years of his life, he traveled, on average, more than two hundred days per year for humanitarian causes. Muhammad Ali, known simply as the greatest, transcended the glamour and glory of being a sports champion to become not only one of the greatest sports figures, but one of the greatest role models of our time. On June third two thousand and sixteen, Muhammad Ali died at the age of seventy-four.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the person who leads the Senate when the Vice President is not there) shall make appropriate arrangements for the presentation, on behalf of Congress, of a gold medal of appropriate design to Muhammad Ali. This is in recognition of his contributions to the United States. For the purpose of the presentation described above, the Secretary of the Treasury (referred to in this law as the Secretary) shall strike a gold medal with suitable emblems, devices, and inscriptions to be determined by the Secretary. Following the presentation of the gold medal in honor of Muhammad Ali, the gold medal shall be given to his wife, Lonnie Ali.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may strike and sell duplicates in bronze of the gold medal struck according to section three. The price must be sufficient to cover the cost of the medals, including labor, materials, dies, use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: Medals struck under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty-one of title thirty-one, United States Code. For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty-four and five thousand one hundred thirty-six of title thirty-one, United States Code, all medals struck under this law shall be considered to be numismatic items (collectible items related to coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: There is authorized to be charged against the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund such amounts as may be necessary to pay for the costs of the medals struck under this law. Amounts received from the sale of duplicate bronze medals authorized under section four shall be deposited into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
